benchmark: Adaptive retrieval — does a system's memory improve held-out retrieval?
why: This is the axis that defines synaptic-*memory*: it is stateful and learns (Hebbian edge reinforcement feeding resonance-ranked search), while OmniFuse was a stateless one-shot retriever. Neither project had measured whether the dynamics improve retrieval QUALITY — synaptic's own memory eval is a contract smoke-gate (eval/scripts/memory_operating_poc.py), not a quality benchmark.

protocol.split: queries split deterministically 50/50 into FEEDBACK (F) and HELD-OUT EVAL (E); E is never searched during the feedback phase
protocol.feedback_signal: identical for both systems — replay F, and for each retrieved top-10 document report whether it was relevant
protocol.metric: MRR@10 on E, before (cold) and after (warm). Report warm - cold.
protocol.leakage_check: zero exact-duplicate and (NFCorpus) zero near-duplicate (Jaccard>=0.8) queries between F and E

conclusion: synaptic's Hebbian reinforcement is neutral-to-harmful on held-out queries in every measurement (-0.0002, -0.0174, -0.0165). OmniFuse's query-conditional memory is positive in every measurement (+0.0019, +0.0076, +0.0618, +0.0729). Cold behaviour is bit-identical to having no feedback at all, so memory can never regress an unused system (verified against the full static suite).
why_hebbian_fails: Reinforcing nodes/edges builds a QUERY-INDEPENDENT prior — 'this document tends to be relevant'. But relevance is a property of a (query, document) pair, not of a document. A prior learned from one topic is noise for another.

our_own_failed_designs.note: We reached the same wall first, from the probabilistic side, and had to abandon it. Recorded because the failure is what pointed at the fix.

beta_posterior_odds.detail: score *= (hits+1)/(misses+1). Every judged document sinks relative to never-shown ones — most retrieved documents are irrelevant, so the judged pool is systematically demoted.

positive_only_odds.detail: score *= (hits+1). Unbounded boost of globally popular documents.

empirical_bayes_lift.detail: shrink the posterior to the corpus base rate so an average document is exactly neutral. Worst of the three — the bias was never the calibration, it was the query-independence.

the_mechanism_that_works.idea: A confirmed query becomes part of what the document is *about*. Memory is stored as text and indexed as its own BM25F field.
the_mechanism_that_works.why_a_separate_field: Appending the query to the body works on a corpus where documents recur across queries (NFCorpus +0.0056) but HURTS one where they do not (MIRACL-ko -0.0134): the body grows and BM25's length normalization penalizes it. A dedicated field has its own length normalization, so an unremembered document is untouched and a remembered one is only lifted when the current query actually overlaps a remembered one.
the_mechanism_that_works.why_not_the_title_field: Reusing the 4x-weighted title slot over-boosts and dilutes the title norm: +0.0009 vs +0.0019 for a dedicated weight-1.0 field.
the_mechanism_that_works.no_tuning: The memory field carries the same weight as the body (1.0). An empty memory field contributes nothing, so the cold store is bit-identical — verified on the full static suite (finreg, 8 public, NFCorpus, MIRACL-ko).

honest_limitations: Feedback is batch: folding memory into the index requires a rebuild (save_index/load_index make that cheap). There is no incremental update yet.

The gain scales with how often documents recur across queries. NFCorpus (82% of relevant docs serve >1 query) gains less in absolute MRR than MIRACL-ko only because its cold MRR is far lower and its queries are harder; the mechanism is not a general accuracy boost.
synaptic was run through its own graph.search + graph.reinforce path (FTS, no embedder). A different synaptic configuration (embedder, EvidenceSearch, consolidation) was not measured.
Absolute cold MRR here is computed on half the queries, so it does not match the static suite's full-set numbers.
